text.title: Database Design Failure Modes
text.summary: 4 antipatterns observed when applying database-design. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix keyed to the methodology.

antipattern.symptom: Service code enforces uniqueness or referential integrity that the DB schema does not.
antipattern.root-cause: Developer relied on ORM validations instead of DDL constraints; bulk import or admin tool bypasses ORM and corrupts data.
antipattern.fix: Add the constraint at DB layer (UNIQUE, FK, CHECK). Re-run the failing import to confirm rejection.

antipattern.symptom: Tables carry many indexes that no current query plan references.
antipattern.root-cause: Indexes added 'just in case' or by ORM auto-migration without measurement.
antipattern.fix: Drop unused indexes (pg_stat_user_indexes shows zero scans). Add a CI check that flags new indexes without a paired query in the workload list.

antipattern.symptom: DROP COLUMN and code change ship in one release; rollback requires both.
antipattern.root-cause: Author treats migration as part of feature, not infra.
antipattern.fix: Split into two releases: release N deploys additive migration + code that handles both shapes; release N+2 deploys destructive migration. Verify with rollback drill.

antipattern.symptom: Critical business rule lives in a database trigger.
antipattern.root-cause: Author wanted an 'always-on' guarantee; trigger felt closer to data than service code.
antipattern.fix: Move logic to application service layer; trigger remains only for structural invariants (audit log, updated_at). Document the move in an ADR.
